module: Module 8: Gene Expression — Transcription and Translation

sections.text: Welcome back to AP Biology — Module Eight, Gene Expression. This is the second half of College Board Unit Six, which lands on roughly twelve to sixteen percent of the AP exam. Today we follow the information in your DNA all the way out to a working protein — transcription, mRNA processing, translation, regulation, and the mutations that break the whole pipeline.

Francis Crick captured it on a single page. DNA, to RNA, to protein. That one arrow is the entire reason your eye cells look different from your bone cells, even though they carry the exact same DNA. One gene, copied into one messenger, translated into one protein — done many times over, in slightly different combinations, and you get every cell type in your body. Today, we trace that arrow end to end.

Three big moves today. First — transcription and mRNA processing, how your DNA gets copied and edited into a finished message. Second — translation at the ribosome, including the codon table, the start and stop codons, and the three steps that build a protein. Third — gene regulation and mutations, how the cell decides which genes to use and what happens when the message is wrong.

The central dogma, written down by Francis Crick. DNA, to RNA, to protein. DNA stores the information in the nucleus. Transcription copies one strand of that DNA into messenger RNA. Translation reads the mRNA, three letters at a time, and builds a protein at the ribosome. There is one famous exception — retroviruses like HIV carry an enzyme called reverse transcriptase that goes backwards, from RNA to DNA. The arrow can run the other way, but only with that special enzyme.

Transcription, eukaryotic version. RNA polymerase Two binds the promoter, marked by the TATA box about twenty-five base pairs upstream, with help from general transcription factors. AP trap — DNA has two strands. The template strand is the one RNA polymerase reads, three-prime to five-prime. The coding strand is its complement — same sequence as the new mRNA except T-for-U. The mRNA is built five-prime to three-prime, antiparallel to the template, using uracil. At the terminator, the transcript releases as pre-mRNA.

Pre-mRNA is not ready to leave the nucleus. Three modifications happen in eukaryotes only. A five-prime cap — a modified guanine — gets stuck on the front, which protects from degradation and helps the ribosome recognize the mRNA. A three-prime poly-A tail — about two hundred adenines — gets added to the back for the same protection job, and to help export it through the nuclear pore. And splicing — the spliceosome, a complex of small nuclear ribonucleoproteins called snRNPs, cuts out the introns and stitches the exons together. Prokaryotes skip all of this — no nucleus, no introns, and transcription and translation happen at the same time, coupled together.

Here is the trick that makes you possible. Your genome has a surprisingly modest gene count — yet your cells produce far more distinct proteins than that number suggests. How? Alternative splicing. A single gene's exons can be mixed and matched in different combinations, so one gene encodes multiple protein isoforms — different versions for different cell types or different developmental stages. Few enough genes that alternative splicing matters. Same DNA, edited differently, different protein.

Translation hardware. The ribosome is built from two subunits — a large sixty-S subunit and a small forty-S subunit. Click them together and you have the eighty-S eukaryotic ribosome. Prokaryotic ribosomes are smaller — seventy-S total. Either way, the ribosome has three slots for tRNAs. The A site receives the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA. The P site holds the peptidyl-tRNA — the one carrying the growing peptide chain. The E site is where empty, used-up tRNAs exit. A, then P, then E — incoming, growing, exit.

The genetic code. mRNA is read in groups of three bases called codons. Four bases, three positions, gives sixty-four codons total. Sixty-one of them code for amino acids. Three are stop codons — U-A-A, U-A-G, U-G-A. And one special codon — A-U-G — is the start codon, and it codes for methionine. Every protein begins life with a methionine at position one. The code is redundant — multiple codons can spell the same amino acid — and nearly universal across all life. Part of that redundancy comes from wobble pairing — the third base of a codon can pair more loosely with the tRNA anticodon, so one tRNA often reads several codons that differ only in the third position.

Try this. The DNA template strand reads three-prime T-A-C-G-G-C-A-T-T five-prime. First, write out the mRNA sequence transcribed from it. Then translate the first three codons into amino acids — use the start codon rule and the codon chart in your head. A-U-G is methionine. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Answer. The mRNA is complementary and antiparallel to the template, so it reads five-prime A-U-G C-C-G U-A-A three-prime. Remember, RNA uses U instead of T. Now read the codons. A-U-G is methionine, the start codon — that is amino acid one. C-C-G codes for proline — amino acid two. U-A-A is a stop codon — translation halts and the release factor pops the chain off. So the finished peptide is just two amino acids long — methionine, then proline. Trap to remember — start codons count as an amino acid; stop codons do not.

Translation in three acts. Initiation in eukaryotes — the small ribosomal subunit binds the five-prime cap of the mRNA, scans down until it finds the first A-U-G, and the large subunit joins. Prokaryotes do it differently — the small subunit binds a Shine-Dalgarno sequence just upstream of A-U-G to position itself. Elongation — the next codon pulls in a matching aminoacyl-tRNA into the A site, and a peptide bond forms between the new amino acid and the chain in the P site. That peptide bond is catalyzed by ribosomal RNA, not protein. The rRNA is a ribozyme — RNA acting as an enzyme. The ribosome translocates one codon, and the tRNAs shift A to P to E. Termination — when a stop codon enters the A site, a release factor binds and the finished polypeptide pops off.

Gene regulation — the cell deciding which genes to actually use. In prokaryotes, genes cluster into operons under one promoter and operator. The lac operon is inducible — normally OFF. When lactose appears, it converts to allolactose, which knocks the repressor off the operator. But there's a second, positive layer the AP loves to ask about — CAP and cAMP. When glucose is scarce, cAMP rises, CAP binds upstream, and transcription gets boosted. So lac runs full blast only when lactose is present AND glucose is low. The trp operon is repressible — normally ON. When tryptophan is abundant, it activates a repressor that shuts the operon OFF. Eukaryotes regulate at many more layers — chromatin remodeling and epigenetics, transcription factors binding enhancers, alternative splicing, and micro-RNAs that degrade or block mRNAs.

Mutations — what goes wrong when a base gets swapped or shifted. Point mutations swap one base. Silent — the new codon still codes for the same amino acid, thanks to redundancy in the code. Missense — codes for a different amino acid; the textbook example is sickle-cell, where DNA G-A-G mutates to G-T-G, glutamate becomes valine at position six of beta-globin, and the hemoglobin clumps under low oxygen. Nonsense — the new codon is a stop codon, so the protein truncates early. And frameshift — an insertion or deletion that is not a multiple of three shifts the reading frame downstream and usually destroys the rest of the protein. Mutations are the ultimate source of new genetic variation that evolution acts on.

Recap. Central dogma — DNA, then mRNA via transcription, then protein via translation. Transcription reads the template strand three-prime to five-prime; the coding strand is the complement and matches the new mRNA except T-for-U. In eukaryotes, mRNA gets a five-prime cap, a poly-A tail, and gets spliced by snRNPs. Alternative splicing is the answer to how a modest gene count produces a vast proteome — same gene, different exon combos. The ribosome has A, P, and E sites; the peptide bond is catalyzed by rRNA — a ribozyme. A-U-G starts; U-A-A, U-A-G, U-G-A stop. Prokaryotes regulate with operons — lac inducible (CAP-cAMP positive layer too), trp repressible. Eukaryotes layer chromatin, transcription factors, splicing, and micro-RNAs. Mutations come in silent, missense, nonsense, and frameshift flavors.

Your path forward. Read OpenStax Biology, Chapters Fifteen and Sixteen, for the deeper version of transcription, translation, and gene regulation. Drill the Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Six problem sets — codon table questions and lac-operon questions are exam staples. Do the assignment in your dashboard. And book an advisor check-in if the codon table or the lac-versus-trp logic still feels fuzzy. Next time, we move into Module Nine — Biotechnology, where we put all of this into practice with PCR, gel electrophoresis, and CRISPR.
